Default reference for general 'how do I grow chile here' questions. [PLACEHOLDER — full publication body to be pasted by user.]
nmsu-h-219: Identification and management of beet curly top virus in chile: vectored by the beet leafhopper. Key symptoms — stunted plants, upward curling and yellowing of young leaves, thickened leathery foliage, plants wilt and die in patches across a field. No cure once infected; management is preventive (vector control, row covers, planting dates). [PLACEHOLDER — full publication body to be pasted by user.]
nmsu-h-240: Phytophthora capsici causes root rot, crown rot, and foliar blight in chile. Symptoms — sudden wilting of healthy-looking plants, dark water-soaked lesions on stems near soil line, plants collapse after irrigation. Favored by warm, wet soil. Management — resistant cultivars, raised beds, careful irrigation, crop rotation, fungicides. [PLACEHOLDER — full publication body to be pasted by user.]
nmsu-a-128: Nutrient management for chile: nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, and micronutrient (iron, zinc) recommendations. Soil tests, application rates, and timing. [PLACEHOLDER — full publication body to be pasted by user.]

nmsu-h-243:
Economic insect infestations in New Mexico chile production have traditionally concentrated in seedling plants, though post-bloom pests can also cause significant damage. Understanding pest identification, life cycles, and management strategies is essential for protecting chile crops from yield and quality losses.

Early season pests present the most frequent management challenges in chile production. Thrips populations are consistently present in New Mexico chile fields, feeding on terminals and buds of young plants. Both nymphs and adults cause expanding leaves to distort and wrinkle through their feeding activity. Heavy infestations may slow seedling development by reducing leaf area available for photosynthesis, particularly during cool growing periods.

Flea beetles represent another significant early season threat. Adult beetles feed on young leaves and cotyledons, typically creating small, round holes in leaf tissue. They may also chew into stems, potentially causing complete seedling loss. Adults overwinter in soil and crop debris, becoming active as spring temperatures rise.

Fleahoppers cause damage by sucking plant sap from leaf undersides, creating small discolored areas that may expand to cause wilting and leaf death under heavy feeding pressure. Both at-planting and foliar insecticide applications effectively reduce fleahopper populations.

Cutworms damage chile by severing seedlings at or near ground level. Larvae feed almost exclusively at night and return to soil during daylight hours. Chile planted adjacent to weedy fields, pastures, or previously weedy areas faces higher cutworm risk.

False chinch bugs damage seedlings by sucking plant juices, causing growth retardation and potential seedling death under heavy infestations. These insects overwinter in small grains and winter annual weeds, migrating to chile fields as their hosts dry.

Post-bloom pests include leafminers, aphids, pod worms, and pepper weevil. Leafminer larvae create winding, light-colored trails just beneath leaf surfaces as they feed within the tissue. Although leafminers are common in New Mexico chile fields, economic infestations remain rare because parasitic wasps naturally control populations.

Green peach aphids feed in terminal buds and leaf undersides, causing distorted and curled leaves under heavy infestations. These pests can indirectly damage chile by transmitting potato Y virus. Natural parasites and predators typically control aphid populations.

The pod worm complex comprises primarily corn earworm and fall armyworm larvae, which feed in and on pods, reducing quality and yield. Larvae bore into pods after feeding on foliage for several days following egg hatch.

Pepper weevil represents the most economically significant post-bloom pest in principal chile-growing regions. Adult weevils deposit eggs within chile buds, flowers, and fruit tissues. Eggs hatch within three to four days, releasing small, cream-colored larvae that burrow into fruit to feed on seeds and placental tissue. Larval feeding discolors the calyx and causes fruit drop.

Economic damage thresholds vary considerably based on chile end-use. Jalapenos destined for whole or sliced market applications have very low tolerance for weevil damage. Management typically requires minimum two pesticide applications spaced seven to ten days apart to disrupt the weevil's life cycle, targeting exposed adults rather than protected larvae within pods.

Effective pest management requires comprehensive field scouting throughout the season. The biological control systems in chile fields are particularly effective, making insecticide selection crucial to avoid secondary pest outbreaks. Control decisions should incorporate chile end-use, days to harvest, infestation levels, secondary pest presence, and harvest crew safety considerations.
nmsu-h-248:
Powdery mildew represents a significant disease challenge for chile pepper growers, particularly in arid and semi-arid regions like New Mexico. The condition is caused by the fungus Leveillula taurica, though its asexual stage, Oidiopsis sicula, is more frequently encountered in New Mexico production areas. When infections develop early in the growing season, they can strip plants of leaves prematurely, causing substantial reductions in both yield and fruit quality.

The pathogen demonstrates remarkable adaptability across a broad range of environmental conditions. The fungus can cause disease in a wide range of environmental conditions from 40 to 95 degrees Fahrenheit and zero to 100 percent relative humidity. However, disease develops most aggressively when temperatures fall between 60 and 80 degrees Fahrenheit with humidity exceeding 85 percent. Spores can germinate and establish infection within 48 hours under favorable circumstances. Wind carries spores to new plant tissues, enabling rapid secondary infections that accelerate disease spread throughout the field.

The disease predominantly affects leaf tissue, though fruit and stems occasionally become infected. Older leaves near the fruit-setting stage are most vulnerable, yet the disease can emerge at any point during the growing season if environmental conditions prove favorable. The fungus maintains an extensive host range that includes cotton, onion, tomatoes, eggplant, and numerous weed species.

The most obvious indicator of powdery mildew infection is the presence of a white, powdery fungal growth that covers the lower leaf surface. This characteristic coating gives the disease its common name. The upper surface of affected leaves typically displays yellowing or browning discoloration. As the infection progresses, leaf edges curl upward, exposing the fungal growth on the leaf underside. Eventually, heavily infected leaves drop from the plant prematurely. This defoliation exposes developing fruit to direct sunlight, potentially resulting in sunscald.

Temperature and humidity work together to determine infection risk. The pathogen requires humidity near 100 percent for spore germination to occur successfully. Once spores germinate, disease development can proceed when humidity levels fall between 35 and 95 percent.

Because the fungus infects such a diverse array of hosts and most chile pepper varieties lack strong resistance, traditional sanitation efforts alone prove insufficient for disease control. While removing infected plant material and controlling weeds around production areas provides some benefit, these measures cannot entirely eliminate disease sources.

Growers typically rely on fungicide applications as the primary management tool. The effectiveness of these sprays depends on early detection and thorough application coverage. Ground application equipment using high-pressure and high-volume sprayers ensures that fungicide penetrates deeply into the plant canopy where lower leaf surfaces harbor the pathogen. A critical management principle involves rotating fungicides with different chemical formulations to prevent resistance.

Key practical takeaways for growers include scouting frequently during the 60 to 80 degree Fahrenheit temperature window with humidity above 85 percent, inspecting undersides of older leaves nearest developing fruit where symptoms first appear, maintaining field sanitation by controlling susceptible weed hosts, initiating fungicide programs at first sign of white powdery growth rather than waiting for defoliation, using high-volume application equipment to ensure coverage of the lower leaf canopy, and rotating fungicide chemistries to delay resistance development. Because defoliation from powdery mildew exposes fruit to sunscald, controlling this disease also protects fruit quality in addition to preserving yield.
nmsu-h-249:
Flower drop represents a significant production challenge in chile pepper cultivation, occurring when buds, flowers, and immature pods abort and fall from plants. Multiple stress conditions can trigger this disorder by interfering with the pollination process. The most common stress factors associated with this disorder are high temperature stress, insufficient water, strong wind, high relative humidity, insect damage and nutrient deficiency, toxicity or imbalances. Growers can minimize losses by maintaining consistent irrigation and fertilization practices during the flowering period.

Sunscald develops on pepper fruits when exposed directly to intense solar radiation, particularly when shaded fruit suddenly receives unobstructed sunlight. The damage can also affect foliage under conditions combining intense sun with high temperatures. Various diseases that cause defoliation or persistent wilting increase susceptibility to sunscald by removing protective leaf cover.

The characteristic symptoms appear only on exposed fruit surfaces as light-colored, sunken, soft and wrinkled areas. Over time, the damaged tissue becomes whitish tan in color and papery in texture. Secondary fungal and bacterial pathogens frequently colonize sunburned areas, accelerating fruit deterioration. While sunscald symptoms may resemble blossom-end rot, sunscald will only occur on the side of the fruit exposed to the sun.

Excessive salt concentrations in soil or irrigation water pose serious threats to pepper production. Because peppers exhibit relative sensitivity to salt, yield reductions begin when the electrical conductivity is greater than 1.5 dS/m. Research conducted in New Mexico demonstrates that a 50 percent yield loss occurs at EC 5.8 dS/m.

Mature plants typically display burned root tips, marginal leaf necrosis, wilt and defoliation when exposed to excessive salt. The problem often intensifies following light rains or minimal irrigation events that redistribute salts into the root zone. Although completely eliminating salt injury remains impractical, strategic cultural practices can substantially reduce damage. Furrow-irrigated peppers should be planted on one side of the bed, while drip-irrigated peppers should occupy bed centers.

Strong winds damage pepper plants through multiple mechanisms. Rapid foliage desiccation and hypocotyl damage prove particularly destructive in young seedlings. Mature plants may experience wilting of desiccated foliage beyond recovery. Physical wind damage includes broken stems and branches. Blowing sand intensifies wind injury by creating necrotic spots on foliage. Protecting seedlings from severe winds can be accomplished through planting into residual stubble from small grain crops or by establishing windbreaks around field perimeters.

Successful direct-seeded chile establishment requires warm soil temperatures. Studies demonstrate that chile seeds germinate optimally at temperatures exceeding 65 degrees Fahrenheit, requiring approximately 11 days to germinate with greater than 80 percent germination at 73 degrees Fahrenheit. Cool temperatures cause sporadic germination, producing uneven and weak stands. Rapid and uniform germination produces vigorous seedlings that resist soilborne diseases.

Severe hail damage results in tattered foliage and pock-marked fruit. Affected tissue frequently becomes chlorotic or necrotic in response to the injury. Fungi and bacteria readily colonize hail-created wounds, accelerating subsequent fruit decay. Prompt removal of heavily damaged plant debris after a hail event and application of protectant fungicides can reduce secondary disease losses. Overall, environmental disorders in chile are managed through matched cultivar selection, consistent irrigation and fertility, windbreaks, salt leaching, and attention to soil temperature at planting.
nmsu-h-250:
Verticillium wilt represents a significant disease threat to chile pepper production across diverse geographic regions. The disease is caused by two soilborne fungal pathogens, Verticillium albo-atrum and Verticillium dahliae. Disease occurrence and severity fluctuate annually and by location, influenced by host susceptibility, pathogen virulence, soil characteristics, and prevailing environmental conditions.

Initial symptoms appear as stunting accompanied by slight yellowing of the lower leaves. As infection advances, the yellowing intensifies and extensive leaf shedding may develop. The severity of visible symptoms depends significantly on soil temperature, air temperature, and nutrient availability within the growing environment.

The pathogenic mechanism involves fungal invasion of xylem tissue, disrupting the plant's water transport system. Vascular discoloration becomes visible as the disease progresses, and plants begin to show wilting symptoms resulting from water stress. Plants may temporarily recover during nighttime hours for several days before permanent wilting and death ultimately occur.

Both Verticillium species demonstrate remarkable versatility in causing disease across numerous plant species. However, these fungi exist as different races or strains with varying virulence levels and host ranges. Notably, the only Verticillium isolates that are consistently unable to infect chile pepper are those from cotton and cabbage.

Verticillium species survive in soil and crop residue as mycelium or microsclerotia, which are resistant resting structures. Microsclerotia tolerate extensive environmental variation, making them effective survival mechanisms. The majority of soil microsclerotia die within two to four years; however, even small surviving populations can produce significant crop damage.

Environmental conditions favoring disease development differ slightly between the two species. Verticillium dahliae prefers warmer temperatures with an optimum around 77 degrees Fahrenheit, while V. albo-atrum functions optimally near 70 degrees Fahrenheit. The chile pepper isolate specifically shows preference for soil temperatures between 85 and 95 degrees Fahrenheit. Both pathogens require adequate moisture for growth and development.

When temperature and moisture conditions favor pathogen activity, root exudates from susceptible plants stimulate microsclerotia germination. The fungus then directly penetrates roots and moves through the root cortex toward the xylem vessels. Fungal colonization plugs the xylem, impairing water and nutrient transport.

Currently, no adequate control measures exist once Verticillium wilt becomes established in a field. Effective management focuses on disease prevention through careful cultural practices. Crop rotations involving three to four years without chile pepper production represent the primary recommended strategy. However, rotation planning becomes complicated due to pathogenic variation among different isolates.

Soil fumigants containing chloropicrin have effectively controlled Verticillium wilt in various crops. Soil solarization also reduces soil populations effectively. Combining fumigation with solarization may increase propagule mortality rates. Although chile pepper cultivars display varying susceptibility levels to Verticillium infection, no commercially available resistant cultivars currently exist.

In summary, growers facing Verticillium wilt should rely on long rotations away from chile and other susceptible solanaceous hosts, maintain aggressive weed control to eliminate alternative hosts, consider soil solarization particularly in smaller plots, avoid importing infested soil or plant debris, and scout regularly so symptomatic plants can be removed before harvest residues build microsclerotial populations further.
nmsu-h-235:
Fresh chiles are highly valued for their distinctive flavors, vibrant colors, and crisp texture, along with being among the richest plant sources of vitamin C. After harvest, these living fruits remain vulnerable to moisture loss, physical damage, and decay while continuing to respire and produce ethylene. These ongoing physiological processes cause quality deterioration and limited shelf life.

Quality begins before harvest through proper variety selection, certified seed, and sound cultural practices including appropriate fertilization, irrigation, and disease management. Crop stress during growth directly influences final yield, pungency, color development, disease incidence, and postharvest performance.

Key quality indicators assessed after harvest include maturity status, freshness level, and freedom from defects such as disease, mechanical injury, insects, blossom-end rot, sunscald, and freeze damage. Fruit firmness or degree of dehydration is the primary indicator of chile freshness. Green chiles lose moisture rapidly after removal from plants and begin shriveling within days without refrigeration.

Maturity assessment relies on physical characteristics including size, shape, color, and texture. Most mature green chiles should be firm and dark green. An ideal New Mexican long green chile displays firmness, smoothness, thick flesh, and a blunt point. Canning-grade green chile should be flat with two locules and approximately six inches long. Jalapenos should feature thick walls, conical shape, glossy green appearance.

During harvest and subsequent handling, chiles soften readily and develop postharvest diseases if bruised or damaged. All varieties, particularly New Mexican green chiles, show high susceptibility to moisture loss, sunscald, and heat damage. These problems emerge rapidly when fruits sit longer than one hour in direct sunlight. Summer-harvested chiles can reach pulp temperatures exceeding 90 degrees Fahrenheit. Therefore, harvesting should occur early morning, with fruits moved to shade and cooled promptly.

Most fresh chile undergoes hand harvesting into buckets or sacks, then transfers to bulk bins for transport to packing facilities or processors. Long green chile fields typically support two harvests, early August and September.

At the shed, chiles are either dry-dumped onto padded ramps or moving conveyor belts, or wet-dumped into chlorinated wash tanks. Daily tank cleaning and frequent chlorination monitoring are essential sanitation practices. Following washing, fruits are sorted to eliminate diseased, overripe, immature, or defective pods before grading.

Storage temperature and container type significantly influence moisture loss. Boxed chiles experience approximately 3.5 percent daily weight loss at 75 degrees Fahrenheit but only 0.5 percent at 46 degrees. Plastic-wrapped trays or polyethylene bags further decrease weight loss, particularly at lower temperatures.

Proper cooling extends shelf life by slowing respiration, moisture loss, color changes, and decay development. Temperatures exceeding 70 degrees Fahrenheit accelerate ripening through enhanced respiration and ethylene output. Preferred cooling methods include room cooling and forced-air cooling.

Optimal storage conditions maintain 45 to 50 degrees Fahrenheit and 90 to 95 percent relative humidity. Lower temperatures cause chilling injury symptoms including softening, pitting, and increased decay susceptibility. Freeze damage occurs at 32 degrees Fahrenheit. Properly cooled fresh chiles store for two to three weeks.

Poor temperature management and ethylene accumulation promote ripening and decay. Since certain fruits and vegetables produce substantially more ethylene than chiles, chiles should never share storage or transport with tomatoes, apples, or melons.
nmsu-h-236:
Dehydrated chile is widely used in prepared meals, seasoning blends, and canning applications. The primary capsicum varieties processed include New Mexican, cayenne, ancho, pasilla, mirasol, piquin, and de arbol types. Quality standards for these products depend heavily on color intensity, pungency levels, and overall flavor profile, making proper processing and storage essential.

Color represents the most critical quality criterion for dehydrated chile products. Paprika is used principally as a coloring agent in foods and its price is directly related to extractable color. Initial color at harvest and color stability throughout processing, shipping, and storage all impact final product value. After harvest, color retention depends primarily on preventing oxidative damage to carotenoid pigments.

Cultivar choice exerts the greatest influence on both color development and pungency characteristics. Nonpungent red chile cultivars commonly grown in New Mexico include NuMex Conquistador, NuMex RNaky, and LB-25. Pungent varieties include New Mexico 6-4, Sandia, and Espanola Improved. Extractable color is measured using methods developed by the American Spice Trade Association.

Field-harvested red chile typically contains between 65 and 80 percent moisture content. This moisture must be reduced to approximately 10 to 11 percent for proper storage. Modern commercial processors employ controlled artificial drying rather than traditional sun-drying methods. At processing facilities, chiles pass through dry-reels, sorting lines, chlorinated washes, rinse stations, and slicing equipment before dehydration. The drying process uses tunnel dryers or continuous belt systems, with temperatures typically ranging from 140 to 150 degrees Fahrenheit.

Color retention during storage depends on several interconnected factors. Cultivar selection and storage temperature exert the greatest influence on preserving color integrity. Ground chile loses color more rapidly than whole pods during storage. Storage conditions significantly impact longevity, as exposure to light and oxygen accelerates pigment bleaching. Recommended storage conditions include temperatures between 32 and 41 degrees Fahrenheit in dark environments.

Antioxidant compounds can enhance color retention during storage. Common additions include ethoxyquin, tocopherols, and ascorbic acid, typically incorporated during product reconditioning phases.

Pungency is another important quality attribute for many dehydrated chile products. Pungency is quantified in Scoville Heat Units or by direct measurement of capsaicinoid concentration using HPLC. Like color, pungency is influenced by genetics and growing conditions, with water stress and high temperatures during pod development tending to increase capsaicinoid content.

Flavor development represents a more complex quality attribute dependent on volatile aromatic compounds that can be lost during drying at excessively high temperatures. Overly aggressive drying can also cause case hardening, where a dry outer shell traps moisture inside pods, leading to non-uniform drying and subsequent microbial growth.

Packaging choices further influence stored product quality. Moisture-impermeable barriers prevent reabsorption of humidity that can drive mold growth, while light-blocking materials or opaque containers slow photo-oxidative color loss. Ground products are often packaged in nitrogen-flushed or vacuum containers to further retard oxidation.
nmsu-h-237:
What most people notice when consuming chile peppers is the characteristic sensation described as heat, bite, or pungency. This sharp, piercing, stinging quality is what draws many consumers to these fruits and spicy foods. Commercial products containing chile peppers are typically labeled with broad categories such as mild, medium, or hot to communicate the intensity consumers can expect.

The burning sensation in chile peppers results from chemical compounds called capsaicinoids. Scientists have identified more than twenty-two of these compounds. Capsaicin and dihydrocapsaicin represent the two dominant types, appearing in the highest concentrations within the fruit.

Two primary factors determine the heat intensity in any given chile pepper plant. First, genetics play a crucial role. Plant breeders can select cultivars to produce fruit with specific heat characteristics. For instance, the cultivar NuMex Joe E. Parker was genetically bred to yield fruit of medium heat. Second, environmental conditions significantly influence capsaicinoid levels. Temperature and water availability both affect heat development. A chile pepper variety bred for mild heat will become noticeably hotter when exposed to field stress. Conversely, a naturally hot cultivar grown under ideal conditions may develop only moderate heat.

Several approaches exist for determining chile pepper heat, ranging from informal to highly scientific. The simplest method involves directly tasting a chile fruit. This approach is quick and inexpensive but highly unreliable since results depend entirely on individual taster perception and sensitivity.

The Scoville Organoleptic Test offers a more refined, systematic approach and was historically the first laboratory technique for measuring heat. Human subjects taste a series of prepared chile samples, with laboratory technicians progressively diluting the samples until tasters can no longer detect heat. Each dilution unit equals one Scoville Heat Unit.

High-Performance Liquid Chromatography, commonly known as HPLC, represents the most accurate measurement method available. The process begins by drying and grinding fruit samples. Researchers then extract the heat-producing chemicals and inject the extract into HPLC equipment for analysis. While more expensive than organoleptic testing, HPLC provides objective, quantifiable results.

For consumers, the Scoville scale provides a widely recognized reference point. Bell peppers measure zero Scoville Heat Units because they lack functional capsaicinoid production. Mild New Mexican long green chile varieties range from roughly 500 to 2,000 Scoville Heat Units, while hotter New Mexican cultivars can reach 5,000 or more. Jalapenos fall in a broad range around 2,500 to 8,000 units, serranos higher still, habaneros in the hundreds of thousands, and the hottest super-hot cultivars exceed one million units.
nmsu-h-257:
New Mexico leads the nation in chile acreage, with red chile and paprika accounting for roughly 40 percent of the state's total production. Red chile comes from New Mexican-type varieties harvested when fruit turns red or reaches physiological maturity. Paprika represents a specific category of low-heat or no-heat red chile varieties distinguished by their high concentration of red pigments called capsanthin and capsorubin found in the fruit walls.

Large red chile processing operations in the Southwest typically maintain their own breeding programs and process proprietary cultivars specifically developed for superior drying characteristics. Smaller growers and home gardeners can successfully use many commercially available New Mexican-type green chile varieties that also produce excellent red, ripe, dried products. The cultivar New Mexico 6-4 functions as a mild option suitable for either green or red chile production. Sandia, a hot variety, works for both green and red uses.

Effective crop rotation represents an essential component of successful red chile cultivation. Chile and other plants in the Solanaceae family, including tomatoes, potatoes, and eggplant, should not occupy the same field more frequently than once every three years. Rotating fields with monocots such as corn, wheat, oats, or sudangrass helps reduce disease and pest pressures associated with chile production.

Water accumulation or pooling encourages rapid spread of chile wilt, caused by the soilborne pathogen Phytophthora capsici. Fields require preparation for thorough drainage, with laser leveling at a grade of 0.01 to 0.03 percent in one or both directions considered optimal. Digging drainage holes at field low points represents a best management practice for quickly removing large water volumes during heavy rainfall periods.

Commercial paprika growers typically direct seed their crops and irrigate through furrow or overhead pivot systems. Given the approximately 165-day growing period required and the desire to begin processing early, fields receive direct seeding early in the season. Planting begins in early March, with the majority of the crop seeded by mid-April.

Many producers plant at high seed rates, typically 4 pounds per acre or greater, to ensure strong stand establishment despite early season diseases and pests affecting seedlings. Fields undergo thinning or blocking to clumps of one to three plants spaced approximately 4 to 6 inches apart. An optimal final stand reaches 40,000 plants per acre.

Nitrogen and phosphorus represent the two nutrients routinely applied to red chile and paprika soils in New Mexico. A soil test conducted before planting helps determine actual nutritional requirements. Chile crops use approximately 200 pounds per acre of nitrogen throughout the growing season. Fertilization typically stops in mid-August to limit new growth and encourage fruit ripening in preparation for red chile harvest.

Harvest commences in late September and continues until completion of the harvest quota or until all red chile is gathered. Mechanical harvesting has achieved widespread adoption, with more than 85 percent of the commercial crop currently harvested by machine.

Maximum paprika yield and quality come from material harvested between October and November, before the season's first freeze below 28 degrees Fahrenheit. While harvesting continues after the first freeze, quality and yields decline significantly. Red chile experiences a 0.5 percent yield loss for every day harvest delays past the optimal window.
nmsu-h-258:
New Mexico's chile production is economically significant, with organic chile commanding premium prices at farmers' markets and restaurants. However, organic production requires a comprehensive approach that addresses soil fertility, pest management, weed control, and disease prevention without synthetic inputs.

Producers generating more than 5,000 dollars annually in chile sales must obtain USDA organic certification to legally market their crop as organic. The certification process requires three years of land management free from prohibited substances. Organic farming relies on practices such as crop rotation, cover crops, composting, and natural pest control methods while prohibiting synthetic materials.

Successful organic chile production begins with careful site selection. Fields should feature well-drained soil that warms quickly in spring, as chile plants are frost-sensitive. Growers must avoid fields that have grown chile or related solanaceous crops like tomatoes, potatoes, or eggplants within the previous three years.

Field preparation involves adding compost to improve water-holding capacity, nutrient retention, and soil aeration. Proper drainage is critical. Cover crops of cereal grains and legumes should be incorporated at least three weeks before planting. A three to five-year crop rotation schedule, including at least one grain crop rotation with chile, supports long-term soil productivity.

Building soil fertility represents one of the most important elements of successful organic production. New Mexico soils typically contain very low organic matter levels. Regularly incorporating composted manure increases fertility, though salt content must be considered since chile seedlings are sensitive to salinity. Legume crops used as green manure effectively increase soil nitrogen through biological fixation.

Chile is a warm-season crop sensitive to frost, so direct seeding or transplanting should be delayed until soil temperatures reach 60 degrees Fahrenheit. In southern New Mexico, optimal direct-seeding occurs between March and May, while central and northern regions should plant four to six weeks later. Direct-seeded fields require a minimum of two pounds of high-quality seed per acre.

Harvest timing depends on pod type. Green chile varieties mature in 50 to 120 days depending on type, while red chile requires approximately 165 days. Green fruit should be harvested when fully sized and firm. Red chile should be allowed to turn completely red and partially or fully dry on the plant before harvest.

Two primary irrigation techniques are drip and furrow methods. Drip irrigation delivers water directly to the root zone with greater efficiency but involves higher expenses. Furrow irrigation, more commonly used by New Mexico growers, involves partially filling furrows between plant rows.

Healthy, fast-growing plants better tolerate weed pressure. Organic weed management begins with proper crop rotation, followed by watering before planting and tilling in the first flush of weeds. Additional methods include organic mulching, cultivation, flame weeding, and approved botanical products.

Phytophthora capsici causes root rot, foliar blight, and fruit rot. Crop rotation with non-host plants and careful irrigation management, eliminating standing water and allowing fields to dry between irrigations, provide effective control.

Verticillium wilt, caused by soil-dwelling fungi, invades the plant's vascular system. Management involves planting disease-free material in uncontaminated fields, crop rotation with non-host plants, biofumigation using Brassica crop residues, and soil solarization.

Beet curly top virus, spread by beet leafhoppers, causes vein swelling, leaf curling, yellowing, and plant stunting. Management includes destroying host weeds like kochia and London rocket, using row covers or insect netting during leafhopper activity.

Early season insect pests include thrips, fall armyworms, root-knot nematodes, and beet leafhoppers. Pepper weevil represents the most economically damaging pest in post-bloom fields. Approved organic pest control methods include row covers as temporary insect barriers, insecticidal soap, Bacillus thuringiensis for caterpillar control, and encouraging beneficial insects through habitat creation and companion planting.
nmsu-h-242:
Tomato spotted wilt virus represents one of the most significant plant diseases affecting agriculture in temperate and subtropical regions. This pathogen has earned recognition for infecting an exceptionally broad range of plant species, demonstrating one of the widest known host ranges for plant viruses with documented infection across over 1,000 species spanning 85 plant families. The disease proves particularly damaging within ornamental and vegetable greenhouse industries.

The virus affects an enormous diversity of cultivated and wild plants. Common vegetable hosts include tomatoes, peppers, potatoes, celery, eggplant, peanuts, lettuce, and various cucurbits. Many legume species show susceptibility, as do numerous ornamental plants. Weeds such as field bindweed, curly dock, and pigweed serve as important wild hosts.

Affected plants display highly variable symptoms depending on infection timing and plant type. The characteristic symptoms that inspired the disease name include young leaves that turn bronze in color and develop numerous small, dark spots. Plants frequently exhibit a wilt-like appearance despite the absence of true wilting mechanisms. Additional foliar symptoms include dieback of growing tips, general stunting, leaf mottling, and dark streaking of the terminal stems.

Fruit symptoms prove particularly striking when infection occurs after flowering. These include chlorotic ring spots and raised bumps on fruit, uneven ripening in ring patterns, and substantial fruit deformation. Infected plants produce poor-quality fruit and have reduced yield. Early-season infections often result in complete failure of fruit production.

The virus depends on thrips insects for transmission between plants. At least ten thrips species facilitate transmission through a persistent propagative mechanism. Once a thrips individual acquires the virus, the virus replicates within the insect and the insect is able to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life.

Controlling this disease presents significant challenges given the broad host range and thrips transmission method. The wide host range, which includes many perennial ornamentals and weeds, enables the virus to successfully overseason from one crop to the next. Thrips populations readily reinfest agricultural fields from untreated surrounding areas.

Greenhouse operations achieve more success managing thrips populations through careful insecticide selection and application timing. Growers should avoid repeated sprays of similar insecticides because thrips are able to build up resistance to commonly used insecticides in a relatively short time. Rotating between different insecticide chemical classes provides superior control.

Cultural practices provide valuable disease reduction strategies when elimination remains impossible. These include beginning with certified virus-free plant material, removing all infected plants since there is no cure once a plant has the disease, controlling weeds that harbor the pathogen, and implementing crop rotations. Some research suggests using reflective mulches under plants may help to reduce infection through disruption of thrips behavior.

Until resistant cultivars become broadly available, growers should rely on an integrated approach that combines clean planting stock, aggressive weed management around fields and greenhouses, physical exclusion of thrips where feasible, judicious and rotated insecticide programs targeting the vector, prompt rogueing of symptomatic plants to limit within-field spread, and reflective mulches in high-risk situations.
nmsu-h-106:
Curly top virus, formally designated as beet curly top virus or BCTV, represents a significant agricultural concern throughout arid and semiarid regions worldwide. The disease occurs commonly across the western United States, extending from Mexico to Canada, and in the eastern Mediterranean Basin. This pathogen demonstrates remarkable host versatility, affecting over 300 species in 44 plant families.

The virus exhibits a preference for broadleaf plants, with no monocotyledonous hosts identified to date. Agricultural and ornamental crops frequently impacted include sugar beets, tomatoes, peppers, beans, potatoes, spinach, and various cucurbits. The disease also establishes itself in numerous weed species, particularly Russian thistle and mustard varieties, which serve as important reservoirs for viral persistence.

Disease symptoms vary considerably depending on the host plant species and the developmental stage at infection. Young seedlings often succumb to infection shortly after exposure. Plants infected after the seedling stage typically survive but display significant stunting and yellowing.

The characteristic symptoms include leaf deformation, pronounced stunting, and yellowing with development of purple veins. Infected foliage becomes thickened, stiff, and crisp, with leaves twisting and curling upward while petioles curve downward. Fruit development suffers markedly, with premature fruit ripening and substantially reduced quality and yield. On tomato and pepper crops, infected fruit appears immature, dull, and wrinkled.

The beet leafhopper transmits curly top virus through a mechanism called persistent transmission. This small insect requires only brief feeding periods on infected plants to acquire the virus. Remarkably, the leafhopper can transmit the pathogen for the remainder of its lifetime after a single feeding event as brief as one minute.

Environmental conditions and insect populations strongly influence disease occurrence and severity. Warm temperatures combined with dense leafhopper populations create particularly favorable conditions for rapid disease spread. Home garden environments often experience more severe problems than commercial fields.

No chemical treatments effectively control curly top virus itself. Disease management therefore relies entirely on cultural and preventive practices. Although genetic resistance has not been identified, certain cultivars demonstrate relative tolerance, making varietal selection a practical strategy.

Fundamental management approaches include planting virus-free transplants, implementing rigorous weed control, eliminating insect populations, and promptly removing diseased plants. Home gardeners can reduce infection pressure by positioning susceptible crops like tomatoes and peppers in partially shaded locations, since leafhoppers prefer to feed in sunny locations.

Physical protection through netting cages provides an effective alternative for young plants. Appropriate mesh size creates a barrier to leafhopper access while providing modest shading.

For commercial growers in the western United States, the most effective strategy is an integrated approach that anticipates leafhopper migration. Regional leafhopper movement from overwintering areas in rangeland and desert weed communities toward irrigated cropland occurs predictably in spring and early summer. Growers can schedule planting dates so that susceptible crops are beyond the most vulnerable seedling stage when leafhoppers arrive.
nmsu-a-609:
Intercropping represents a multiple cropping system where two or more crops are grown at the same time on one field. Relay intercropping specifically involves planting a second crop into a field when the initial crop has reached its reproductive phase but has not yet achieved physiological maturity. This approach allows farmers to produce two distinct crops within a single growing season, particularly in areas where the growing period is too limited for traditional double cropping systems.

Forage brassicas offer considerable promise as secondary crops in high-desert regions of the southwestern United States because they tolerate frost well. When relay intercropped into frost-sensitive crops like chile and sweet corn, brassicas can potentially deliver two harvests annually while boosting overall productivity per acre. These plants deliver additional benefits beyond yield potential, including elevated protein levels, excellent forage quality, and strong digestibility characteristics that make them valuable for livestock feed.

Several brassica varieties have demonstrated promise as forage crops, including kale, radish, rape or canola, and turnip species. Researchers at the Alcalde Sustainable Agriculture Science Center undertook systematic studies from 1993 through 1995 to evaluate how four different brassica types affected yields of both chile and sweet corn under high-desert growing conditions in north-central New Mexico.

The researchers seeded brassicas at specific rates depending on variety. Eight pounds per acre for rape and kale seed and 4 pounds per acre for turnip seed were the interseeding rates applied. Two distinct timing windows were tested. In sweet corn, early interseeding occurred at the V7-V9 growth stage, while late interseeding happened at the blister stage. In chile fields, early interseeding took place when plants reached 12 to 16 inches in height.

Harvest schedules varied by crop type. Green chile was collected between mid-September and early October. Sweet corn was harvested at the milk stage in mid to late August. Brassica forage was harvested roughly 60 days after the primary crop, typically in mid-November.

Results from sweet corn intercropping showed that neither the timing of brassica seeding nor the particular brassica species significantly reduced corn yields compared to corn grown alone. Early interseeding consistently produced substantially greater forage yields than late plantings. Rondo turnips delivered the maximum forage yield at approximately 6,040 pounds per acre of dry matter, followed by Forage Star turnips at roughly 4,685 pounds per acre.

In the chile intercropping experiments, early interseeding again produced significantly superior forage yields relative to late planting windows. Forage Star turnips achieved the highest yields at approximately 1,700 pounds per acre, with Rondo turnips producing around 1,160 pounds per acre.

The data consistently demonstrated that neither intercropping practice nor brassica variety selection negatively impacted primary crop performance. The chile yields were unaffected by brassica presence, and sweet corn yields similarly remained strong with interseeded brassicas growing beneath and around the primary plants.

Based on the research findings, the Rondo and Forage Star turnip varieties showed the most reliable performance across all tested conditions. These results suggest that in the Alcalde production region and comparable high-desert areas, interseeding turnips into chile and sweet corn fields presents viable potential for producing fall forage suitable for livestock grazing. This relay intercropping approach addresses regional constraints effectively, allowing farmers to maximize seasonal productivity in areas where traditional double-cropping remains impractical due to climate limitations.
nmsu-h-216:
Knowing the proper harvest time for vegetables ensures optimal flavor, texture, and nutritional quality. This guide provides specific guidelines for common garden vegetables grown in New Mexico.

Asparagus should be cut or snapped off at soil level when spears reach six to ten inches in length, before the heads open. Stop harvesting once the average spear diameter falls below one-quarter inch. Lima beans are ready when the pods feel well filled but not overmature, with seeds remaining green and tender. Snap beans should be harvested at full size when the beans are about one-quarter of their mature size.

Beets reach ideal size at one and one-half to two inches in diameter. Harvest them before hot spring weather or moderate fall frosts arrive. Broccoli heads should be fully developed and tight before flowers begin opening. Use a sharp knife and remove the heads while leaving four to six inches of stem attached. Brussels sprouts mature from the bottom of the plant upward. Select sprouts that are bright green, plump, and firm before hard freezes occur. These sprouts develop a sweeter taste if left on the plant until after a mild frost.

Cabbage varieties have different harvest requirements. Standard cabbage should form compact, firm heads, though heads may burst during hot weather. Cantaloupes reach full maturity when the stem separates easily from the fruit. Carrots vary by variety but are typically ready at three-quarters to one and one-half inches in diameter. Cauliflower requires special attention: tie or break outer leaves over the developing head when it reaches one to two inches in diameter to protect it from sunlight. Check the curds every two to three days and harvest when the head is full-sized, white, tight, and smooth rather than ricey.

Swiss chard can be harvested continuously as leaves become large enough. Collards, kale, and mustard greens should be young and tender, harvested as whole young plants or lower leaves from older plants. Sweet corn is ready when the kernels reach near their full size at the milk stage, with brown silks visible.

Cucumbers intended for pickling can be harvested at any desired size. Slicing cucumbers should be picked before the seeds reach half-size. Eggplants should be harvested while immature or less than fully grown, remaining firm with a glossy black or purple color. Lettuce heads are ready when they become solid and yield slightly to gentle pressure. Okra pods should be immature, measuring three to four inches long.

Bulbing onions are ready when about half the tops have weakened and fallen over and the bulbs reach at least two inches in diameter. English peas should be bright green with fully developed but still tender pods, before seeds develop completely. Bell and sweet peppers should be full-sized, firm, and crisp. Green chile peppers should be full-sized, firm, and crisp when squeezed, harvested while green or with slight red color. Red chile peppers should be left on the plant until completely red and partially to fully dried.

Irish potatoes should not be harvested until the vines turn yellow or die, since tubers continue growing until that point. Sweet potatoes must be dug before freezing weather arrives, then cured under warm conditions between eighty to eighty-five degrees Fahrenheit for one week. Pumpkins should be fully colored with skins hard enough to resist denting with a fingernail. Radishes are ready as soon as the roots reach edible size.

Spinach can be cut just below the crown for a single harvest, or above the growing point to allow regrowth. Summer squash should be harvested as immature fruit when young and tender. Winter squash requires a firm, glossy rind that resists fingernail denting. Tomatoes can be picked at the pink stage or beyond while still firm. Turnips reach ideal size at one and one-half to three inches in diameter. Watermelons show several ripeness indicators: the tendril opposite the fruit withers, the soil spot changes from white to creamy yellow, the rind resists fingernail denting, and tapping produces a dull rather than hollow sound.
nmsu-h-220:
An early start represents an important factor for both home and market gardeners, and several methods can help achieve this advantage. The most critical principle to remember is that plants require gradual exposure to an unprotected environment. Shading and proper watering become essential tools in the hardening process for these plants.

Peat pots, peat pellets, and seed flats can be seeded indoors to start plants, but indoor space often becomes limited quickly in an active household. For this reason, starting plants outdoors proves more practical for many gardeners.

Several techniques can hasten plant growth during periods when ambient temperatures fall below what most plants prefer. Selecting appropriate varieties matters significantly, as some perform better in cool weather conditions than others. Short-season sweet corn cultivars outperform mid or late-season varieties. Frost-tolerant cole crops can be started even earlier in the season.

Properly placed fertilizer in sufficient quantities promotes early and vigorous growth. Irrigation plays an equally important role in this process. Moist soil surfaces tend to be cooler than dry ones due to evaporation, though drip irrigation appears to result in faster growth than other methods.

Most mulches, excluding plastic films, actually keep soil cooler because of their insulating qualities. Placing seeds on the south side of east-west oriented beds promotes faster early growth, as southern exposures warm more quickly from solar radiation.

Cold frames provide protection from moderately cold temperatures and trap solar energy efficiently when properly oriented. These box-like structures feature a sloping cover or lid, which may be hinged to allow easy ventilation. The sloping cover should face south, southwest, or west to capture maximum solar energy. The height difference between the back and front should measure between three and four inches for each foot of distance between them.

Hot beds follow the same design as cold frames but include an added heat source. Heating cables, available through seed catalogs, can be successfully buried in the soil medium. Manure hot beds have been used traditionally, with decomposing material providing the heat source. A layer of manure measuring twelve to twenty-four inches deep should be placed with an eight to twelve-inch layer of growing medium on top.

Hot caps and walls of water represent small, greenhouse-like conical devices for protecting individual plants. Made of translucent paper or plastic film fused to form water-filled tubes, they require ground anchoring. Since they typically reach only twelve to eighteen inches in height, plants eventually outgrow them.

Tunnel cloches can be easily constructed using wire loops forming semicircles above ground and covered with non-opaque plastic films. Clear translucent plastic or spun-bonded row cover fabric can cover these structures. Clear plastic captures more heat but risks overheating plants, while white plastic gains less heat but prevents frost damage.

Black plastic mulch, a film measuring four to six mils thick, provides seedlings with an early start. The film is placed over prepared seed beds and anchored at the sides. Seeds or transplants are placed through holes made in the plastic. The black color absorbs solar energy and heats the soil beneath, while minimizing weed problems.

Water-filled inner tubes trap solar energy and release heat during nighttime, offering freeze protection. One-gallon plastic jugs with cut-off bottoms can be pushed into soil deep enough to prevent blowing over. Holes dug in the ground can start plants like tomatoes, covered with transparent material to admit light. Soil must be added as plants grow, gradually filling the holes.
nmsu-h-246:
Community vegetable gardening has emerged as a valuable approach to addressing food security challenges. With significant portions of New Mexico's population experiencing food insecurity, these gardens provide opportunities for apartment residents, lower-income families, and seniors to grow fresh produce while building community connections.

Community gardens take several forms. Individual or family plot systems typically charge fees for gardeners who wish to grow their own vegetables. Community support gardens utilize volunteers or other laborers to produce food benefiting food banks, shelters, or missions. School-based projects emphasize educational outcomes. Some gardens incorporate Community Supported Agriculture models where participants pay annual fees to receive set produce quantities from garden associations.

Establishing a successful community garden begins with organizing interested community members to assess genuine local need and commitment. The planning committee should develop a survey to gauge community interest, measure commitment levels, and identify who will benefit most.

The selection of a coordinator proves crucial to success. This person must possess time availability, patience, leadership skills, and creativity. Horticultural background and the ability to work with government officials and business leaders are essential. Local government employees and retirees prove ideal.

Site selection requires careful consideration beginning at least six to eight months before planting. Government-owned lands offer advantages since they face minimal risk of being sold. Private property requires confirmation that the owner holds clear title without liens. Any lease should extend at least three to four years given potential improvement investments.

Sites must be avoided if they show signs of industrial contamination. The ideal location is level with fertile, well-drained soil. Gardens should not be positioned where large trees will create excessive shade or root competition. Sites should receive at least eight hours of direct sunlight daily. Access to quality water with appropriate water rights is essential.

Fall soil testing reveals nutrient deficiencies and identifies potential heavy metal contamination. A water quality test should accompany soil analysis.

Garden organizational structures should balance simplicity with necessary functions. Guidelines and rules should be agreed upon before gardening begins. Most successful gardens establish clear policies addressing plot registration, fees, maintenance responsibilities, pest management restrictions, and behavioral expectations.

Participants should commit to approximately one hour weekly toward community garden maintenance. Tall-growing crops should not shade neighboring plots, and vining plants must remain contained. Cleanup deposits encourage plot restoration at season's end.

Plot sizes typically range from 144 square feet to 750 square feet depending on garden scope. National averages suggest 700-square-foot plots can supply vegetable needs for a family of four throughout the growing season. New sites benefit from initial commercial tractor tilling to address hardpan and drainage issues.

Raised beds constructed with boards or bricks help address poor soil conditions. Pathway mulching with sawdust or bark controls weeds and prevents mud. Pathways should accommodate wheelbarrows and equipment, requiring minimum three-foot widths.

Organic or pesticide-free management approaches minimize liability and philosophical conflicts among participants. Vining crops should be trained on trellises; tomatoes secured to cages. Succession planting maximizes productivity within limited space.

Cover crops planted in fall, such as winter wheat, rye, oats, or barley, improve soil health and neighborhood aesthetics during winter months. These green manure crops should be incorporated into soil at least one month before spring vegetable planting begins.

Vandalism presents ongoing challenges. Fencing deters some problems. Involving youth in garden ownership, maintaining regular coordinator visits, and promptly harvesting ripe fruit reduce theft temptation.
nmsu-h-251:
Protected agriculture encompasses methods designed to alter a plant's growing environment to enhance development. One widely adopted technique among commercial growers involves row covers, which are flexible materials made from transparent or semitransparent substances. These covers enclose plant rows to boost crop productivity by raising both air and soil temperatures while diminishing wind-related damage.

Depending on material thickness, row covers can provide frost protection ranging from 2 to 7 degrees Fahrenheit. Heavier spunbonded polypropylene materials offer superior frost defense but reduce light penetration. The greenhouse effect created by these covers warms surrounding air and soil, promoting seed germination and root development. When paired with black plastic mulch, many crops mature one to three weeks ahead of conventional cultivation. Cucurbits such as squash and cucumbers show particularly strong responses, with yield increases reaching 25 percent.

Wind protection represents another significant advantage. Row covers supported by wire hoops shield plants from wind, blowing sand, hail, and heavy rain, reducing plant stress and desiccation of tender young growth. Lightweight floating covers protect crops from insects when edges are sealed with soil, decreasing insecticide requirements and disease transmission.

Cost represents the primary disadvantage. Expenses vary based on crop type, row spacing, and cover materials. Wire hoops for supported covers incur greater costs than floating systems. Labor requirements increase substantially, particularly with hoop-supported tunnels needing setup, maintenance, and potential removal for weeding, pesticide application, or pollination.

Pest management becomes complicated because insects entering covered areas multiply rapidly in the favorable environment. Weeds flourish without herbicide treatment or plastic mulch, sometimes requiring cover removal for hand weeding. Flowering crops like cucurbits and strawberries need cover removal during bloom to enable bee pollination.

Three primary row cover approaches dominate commercial production. Plastic-covered trenches involve stretching polyethylene across a trench approximately 7 inches deep, with soil mounded along edges for tension. Pre-emergence herbicide application controls weeds.

Floating row covers, manufactured from spunbonded polyester or polypropylene, come in widths from 5 to 50 feet. Weights range from 0.3 to 2.0 ounces per square yard. Covers weighing 0.5 to 1.25 ounces per square yard provide 2 to 6 degrees Fahrenheit of frost protection while transmitting approximately 70 percent of light. Apply floating covers loosely over crops and secure edges with fabric pins and soil. Avoid applying floating covers over single-stemmed crops like peppers, tomatoes, eggplant, and squash, as wind can break stems.

Hoop-supported row covers or low tunnels employ wire hoops made from 9- to 10-gauge galvanized wire, typically 48 to 60 inches long and spaced 4 to 5 feet apart, shaped into arches. The California System uses two sheets of 36-inch-wide, 1.5-mil clear polyethylene supported by hoops.

Perforated plastic tunnels solve venting challenges by incorporating 3/8-inch perforations, allowing ventilation without removing covers and reducing wind damage. Spunbonded fabric tunnels represent another popular option, requiring no venting and resisting tears better than polyethylene.

Row covers typically deploy three to four weeks before normal planting to encourage growth or provide frost protection. Remove covers at bloom for fruiting crops requiring bee or wind pollination. Cool-season crops may need removal later when higher temperatures reduce quality.
nmsu-h-252:
If you are an enthusiastic vegetable gardener ready to expand your growing capacity, installing a hoop house offers a practical pathway forward. These structures use solar energy and natural air movement to passively modify your garden's microclimate, extending growing seasons for warm-weather crops while protecting cool-season vegetables throughout winter months.

A hoop house differs fundamentally from a greenhouse in that it relies on the sun's solar energy and natural air movement to passively heat and cool the structure rather than mechanical systems. Crops are typically planted directly in soil, though raised beds, grow bags, and containers work equally well. The primary advantage lies in economics: construction costs run approximately two to three dollars per square foot, with minimal maintenance once completed.

Before breaking ground, evaluate several critical factors. Light penetration matters enormously since plants require adequate sunlight. In regions prone to heavy snow or strong winds, your structure must withstand significant load stress. Plan the interior height to allow comfortable working and movement during the growing season.

Temperature management depends entirely on solar heating. Install a maximum-minimum thermometer to track seasonal variations and daily fluctuations. Without supplemental heat, plants can freeze when outside temperatures drop excessively. During summer months, excessive heat requires active management through ventilation by opening doors, venting through the top, or rolling up the sides.

Water availability proves absolutely essential. Whether using overhead sprinklers, drip irrigation, soaker hoses, or manual application, ensure a reliable year-round supply. Southwestern irrigation water often contains elevated dissolved salts. Unlike outdoor gardens where rainfall leaches salts deeper into soil, hoop house coverings exclude rain, causing salt buildup.

Choose a moderately level site with good drainage and quality soil. Locate it in an open area where trees and obstacles will not obstruct sunlight. Consider wind protection and proximity to water and electrical sources.

Orientation significantly impacts productivity. An east-west alignment maximizes passive winter warming, potentially enabling midwinter production rather than mere season extension. North-south orientation facilitates summer ventilation by capturing south and southwest air currents that remove hot air accumulation.

Warm-season vegetables like tomatoes, cucumbers, peppers, and squash are highly frost-sensitive. Plant these varieties 4 to 5 weeks before your average last spring frost date. Cucurbits require pollinating insects for fruit set.

Cool-season crops including lettuce, spinach, carrots, and broccoli thrive at lower temperatures and suffer in excessive heat. Plant these approximately 4 to 5 weeks before your average first fall frost date. Carrots develop poor color below 50 degrees. Lettuce may become bitter and bolt prematurely in heat.

The sheltered hoop house microclimate may actually favor certain pests and diseases. Protected pests lose exposure to natural predators and freezing temperatures that would normally control populations. Establish regular scouting schedules and implement crop rotation to identify problems early. Environmental stress from temperature extremes or inconsistent watering can trigger physical plant disorders.

Beneficial insects play crucial roles in maintaining ecological balance. Encourage their presence or introduce them deliberately to control harmful species.
nmsu-h-261:
Growing high-quality vegetables in New Mexico requires intensive management of many factors including scarce water supplies, weed and pest control, nutrient application, and disease management. Using mulches can help farmers and gardeners reduce the impact of these challenges by placing organic or synthetic materials on soil around plants to create a more favorable growing environment.

Mulching serves numerous purposes for vegetable production. It reduces soil moisture evaporation and ensures more even soil moisture supply, which is particularly valuable in New Mexico's arid climate. Mulches reduce or prevent weed growth, insulate soil from extreme temperature changes, prevent mud from splashing on crops, reduce fruit rots in melons, strawberries, and tomatoes, reduce soil crusting and erosion, minimize soil compaction, and protect perennial plants from freezing.

A good mulch should be non-toxic to plants, easy to apply, free from disease and weed seed, and not so absorbent that it removes moisture from plants. It should be easy to remove or biodegradable once incorporated into soil.

Organic mulches like hay, straw, grass clippings, and compost are traditionally used in backyard gardens and smaller operations. They provide many benefits of synthetic mulches except soil warming and perennial weed control. Unlike synthetic options, organic mulches return nutrients to soil through decomposition.

Well-rotted or composted manure mixed with straw provides good results, though careful attention to weed seed is necessary. Leaves work best placed around vegetables and on bare plots as they fall, preferably shredded to prevent packing. Pine needles make excellent mulch especially for acid-loving plants like strawberries. Straw serves as good general mulch for winter protection and between vegetable rows.

Synthetic mulch options include cloth materials, newspaper applied three to six sheets thick, paper mulches biodegradable for end-of-season incorporation, plastic film mulches made of polyethylene, and woven weed barriers. Plastic film mulches vary in thickness and may be smooth or embossed.

Most coarse natural organic mulches should be applied two to three inches deep over the entire mulched area. Grass clippings should dry before application and never exceed one inch depth. Woody materials should not be incorporated into soil as they tie up nitrogen.

Plastic mulches for home gardens should be perforated for air and water movement. Commercial plastic and paper mulches are applied by hand in smaller operations or by machine in larger ones. Beds normally have five- to six-foot centers, are four to six inches high and thirty to thirty-four inches wide.

Drip lines can be installed on soil surface under mulch or two to three inches below surface. For crops like tomatoes, chile, and vine crops, drip lines should be located four to eight inches to the side of the crop.

Clear and black plastic mulches applied early in spring warm soil, with black plastic also deterring weed growth while clear plastic permits it. In hot climates, paper mulch heats soil less than black plastic. Natural organic mulches and white plastic applied in summer cool soils, important for heat-sensitive crops like strawberries. Silver reflective mulches and aluminum foil cool soils while reflecting light to repel aphids.
nmsu-h-262:
Plant breeding combines art and science. Before commercial seed companies existed, farmers and home gardeners saved seeds annually to ensure crops for the next season. Understanding a plant's biological classification, flower biology, and pollination mechanisms allows modern seed savers to incorporate scientific principles into their work.

Saving your own seeds offers several advantages. You can reproduce varieties that thrive in your specific growing area, preserve rare or excellent varieties for long-term survival, adapt plants to your micro-environment, and reduce costs by avoiding yearly commercial seed purchases.

Critical knowledge includes understanding a plant's genus and species to identify what other varieties or relatives will successfully cross-pollinate with it. Hybrid plants result from specific crosses between two distinct inbred lines and produce exceptional first-generation offspring. However, seed saved from hybrids will not produce uniform plants.

Biennial crops require two years to produce seed. Biennial vegetables include onions, carrots, beets, cabbage, and kale. Avoid saving seed from plants that bolt during the first season. Annual crops produce seed in one season.

Flowers contain male parts, female parts, or both. Perfect flowers contain both male and female structures. Imperfect flowers contain only male or female parts.

Self-pollination occurs when a perfect flower pollinates itself or when male flowers on one plant pollinate female flowers on the same plant. Cross-pollination occurs between flowers on different compatible plants. Chile peppers produce perfect flowers and readily self-pollinate. Squash produces imperfect flowers, so pollen from male flowers must reach female flowers.

Maintaining variety purity requires careful management. Several methods separate different varieties of the same species. Distance isolation separates varieties geographically. Wind-pollinated corn requires two-mile separation between varieties, while insect-pollinated zucchini needs half-mile separation. Time isolation staggers pollination by planting varieties with different maturity dates. Physical barriers include blossom bags or seed cages.

Population size affects genetic diversity and inbreeding depression. Corn is particularly susceptible to inbreeding depression if seed comes from too few plants. For corn, collect from at least one hundred plants, preferably two hundred or more.

Seed cleaning uses two methods. Wet processing handles seeds within fruit flesh, like tomatoes, cucumbers, and melons. Tomato seeds can ferment by placing seeds and pulp in a container, stirring daily for two to three days. Dry processing handles seeds from dried pods or husks.

Store seeds in cool, dark, dry conditions below fifty degrees Fahrenheit with humidity of fifty percent or less. Refrigerators provide ideal conditions. Most properly stored seeds remain viable for three to five years.

Beans self-pollinate, making them excellent beginner crops. Tomatoes usually self-pollinate but may show exerted stigmas allowing insect cross-pollination. Chile peppers easily self-pollinate but tolerate insect cross-pollination within species. Squash varieties within the same species easily cross-pollinate via insects. Corn cross-pollinates via windborne pollen traveling up to two miles.
nmsu-h-180:
Hydroponics represents a cultivation method where plants grow without soil, instead utilizing mineral nutrient solutions dissolved in water. The approach bathes plant roots in highly oxygenated, nutrient-rich water, sometimes supported by inert media like gravel or perlite. This technique produces significantly faster growth rates compared to soil-based cultivation. Most importantly for arid regions like New Mexico, hydroponics consumes substantially less water than traditional agriculture.

These systems typically operate within controlled indoor environments such as greenhouses, which reduces land requirements and enables location in urban spaces with limited available area. A New Mexico chile variety called NuMex Espanola Improved became the first fruit harvested in space using hydroponic systems in October 2021.

Every hydroponic system incorporates three essential components: a nutrient reservoir containing water with properly balanced nutrient concentrations, a distribution system for circulating systems, and a plant growth platform supporting roots to bathe in the nutrient solution.

Nutrient solutions must contain sixteen essential elements for plant growth. Growers often purchase pre-blended concentrated A and B formulations kept separate to prevent precipitation. After preparation, solutions require pH adjustment to the 5.5 to 6.5 range preferred by most hydroponic crops.

Growing media in hydroponic systems provide structural root support and maintain essential water-to-oxygen ratios. Popular options include rockwool with excellent water retention but requiring pH pre-soaking, Oasis plugs that arrive pH-balanced, compostable polymer-based peat and coco plugs, coconut coir offering good water retention, perlite providing lightweight aeration, vermiculite retaining moisture, recycled glass growstone, and LECA clay aggregates.

Hydroponic systems fall into open or closed categories. Open systems introduce fresh nutrient solution with each irrigation cycle through drip systems, allowing excess water and nutrients to drain as waste. Bag or slab culture systems exemplify this approach, growing plants in media-filled bags.

Closed systems recirculate nutrient solutions continuously with no wastewater discharge, offering immense water savings and reduced fertilizer costs. Non-recirculating closed systems include wick systems that passively deliver water via capillary action, and the Kratky system where plants suspend above nutrient reservoirs. Both work well for small, fast-growing, non-fruiting plants like lettuce and herbs.

Recirculating closed systems offer greater versatility. Ebb and flow systems periodically flood growing trays with pumped nutrient solution that returns to reservoirs by gravity. Deep water culture suspends plants on floating polystyrene rafts above oxygen-rich nutrient tanks. Nutrient film technique systems use shallow nutrient water streams recirculated past roots in watertight channels.

Aeroponics represents another option where plants develop in channels with nutrient solution periodically misted onto roots. No media support plants after seedling transfer. These systems suit propagation and medicinal root crop production but require regular monitoring to prevent emitter clogging.

Hydroponics offers remarkable advantages for New Mexico's agricultural challenges. The reduced water consumption makes it invaluable in arid climates while the controlled environment approach produces consistently high-quality, safe food for local communities.
nmsu-h-176:
Plants in New Mexico's southwestern climate face significant stress from non-living factors including temperature extremes, minimal precipitation, and poor soil quality. These environmental challenges make plants more susceptible to insect pest infestations. While pest damage can reduce crop yield and plant vigor, most insects are harmless and many provide beneficial services through pollination, predation on pests, and nutrient cycling.

A successful IPM program combines multiple control strategies to reduce reliance on chemical applications. The foundation begins with four key steps: prevention through proper plant selection and care, monitoring to detect pest presence and assess damage levels, accurate identification of both host plants and pests, and selection of appropriate management tactics.

Cultural control modifies the garden environment to become less favorable for pests. Selecting resistant plant varieties, practicing good sanitation, preventing plant stress through proper site selection and watering, and rotating crops all contribute to reducing pest pressure.

Mechanical control uses physical barriers and direct removal methods. Row covers protect plants from pests. Hand removal, high-pressure water sprays to dislodge pests, and pruning out infested material are also effective mechanical strategies.

Biological control introduces or encourages living organisms to suppress pest populations. This can involve planting diverse floral resources to support natural enemy populations, providing overwintering habitat, and when necessary, introducing commercially available beneficial insects. Natural enemies include predators like lady beetles and lacewings, parasitoid wasps, and pathogenic fungi.

Chemical control should be employed only when pest populations reach economic threshold. When using chemicals, gardeners should follow label instructions precisely, apply products during appropriate pest life stages, avoid spraying during pollination times, and consider selective products like Bt that minimize harm to beneficial insects. Organic options including neem oil, insecticidal soaps, and spinosad provide alternatives.

Aphids cluster on new growth and transmit plant viruses. These soft-bodied insects can be managed through water sprays, row covers, or by releasing natural enemies. Bagrada bugs feed on mustard family plants causing starburst-shaped lesions. Hand-picking and row covers provide mechanical control.

Squash bugs attack cucurbit crops, causing yellowing and wilting. Monitoring leaf undersides for characteristic clusters of bronze eggs allows for crushing before nymphs emerge. Thrips cause thin white lines on foliage and transmit tomato spotted wilt virus.

Corn earworms bore into corn ears and tomato fruits. Monitoring with pheromone or black-light traps helps time applications of Bt or neem oil. Cucumber beetles vector bacterial wilt disease.

Cutworm larvae girdle plants at soil level, particularly in spring. Codling moths damage apple and pear fruit by boring to the seed cavity. Peachtree borers tunnel in stone fruit trunks.

Natural enemies are essential to IPM success. Parasitoid wasps from multiple families attack pest eggs, larvae, and pupae. Tachinid flies parasitize many pest larvae. Predaceous insects including lady beetles, lacewings, ground beetles, spiders, and minute pirate bugs consume multiple prey items throughout their lives.
nmsu-h-181:
Beneficial insects including bees, wasps, butterflies, moths, flies, and beetles provide critical ecosystem services through pollination and natural pest control in home gardens and agricultural settings. Over eighty percent of fruits, vegetables, and native plants depend on pollinators for reproduction. Unfortunately, pollinator populations face significant decline due to habitat loss and fragmentation, pathogens and parasites, pesticide use, pollution, and climate change.

To support wild pollinator populations, home gardeners and small-scale farmers can create suitable habitat. All pollinators require three fundamental elements: food, shelter, and water. Planting diverse flowers that bloom across different seasons provides essential nectar and pollen resources. Lepidopteran pollinators such as butterflies and moths require host plants for their caterpillar stage.
