Drip systems offer increased yields, reduced labor requirements, lower fertilizer and pesticide usage, and automation opportunities with fewer tractor passes through fields. Water is applied directly to the root zone so less is lost to deep percolation and evaporation. However, installation costs run substantially higher than conventional systems, typically ranging from 700 to 1,200 dollars per acre. Systems demand skilled labor, careful design for uniform water distribution, and intensive maintenance including filtering, acidification, chlorination, and flushing to prevent clogging.
Regular upkeep includes backwashing filters periodically, flushing main lines and laterals biweekly or as needed based on water quality, and applying chlorine treatments. Chlorine must be administered frequently to subsurface tubes, regardless of the water quality, to control bacterial slimes and algae that can plug emitters. Sundance Farms successfully applied biweekly chlorine at 7 parts per million with acidified water at approximately five dollars per acre annually.
Phosphorus applications typically occur before planting through banded applications near developing seedlings, with 80 to 120 pounds of P2O5 per acre recommended for soils testing below 10 parts per million. Potassium requirements increase under drip irrigation due to concentrated root systems in smaller soil volumes. Seasonal nitrogen requirements range from 150 to 250 pounds per acre, with most nitrogen applied during the 8 to 10 weeks following first flowering when crop uptake accelerates. Fertigation through the drip system spoon-feeds nutrients, improving efficiency and reducing leaching losses.
Tensiometers measure soil moisture at specific depths, with optimal moisture for clay loam soils around 25 centibars. Placement at multiple depths within the active root zone provides a more complete picture of soil moisture conditions. Nitrate meters assess nitrogen status in petiole sap, guiding fertigation timing and rates. The sufficiency zone for vegetative chile growth measures 900 to 1,400 parts per million nitrate-nitrogen, declining to 500 to 800 parts per million during early fruit development. Monitoring electrical conductivity of the soil solution also helps detect salt buildup that drip systems can cause at the wetting-front edges.
Several injection device options exist, including differential pressure or batch tanks offering simplicity but declining concentration over time, venturi devices providing moderate consistency at lower cost, positive displacement pumps delivering precise constant rates, and centrifugal pumps suited for higher flow applications. Injection points should be located downstream of filters for water mixing before distribution. Backflow prevention is essential to protect the water source from contamination.
Analysis comparing drip to furrow irrigation shows drip systems producing approximately 12 percent greater net operating profit despite higher capital and fixed costs. Yield increases of 25 percent, reduced chemical and fertilizer requirements of 18 and 26 percent respectively, and lower seed costs of 20 percent offset the 47 percent higher capital investment and 19 percent increase in fixed costs.
Successful adoption requires understanding local water characteristics, designing systems with adequate filtration and chemical treatment capacity, establishing appropriate maintenance schedules, and monitoring crop nutrient status throughout the season. Proper planning of lateral length, emitter spacing, and flow rates ensures uniform water distribution across the field. The Rincon Farm installation invested fifty-two thousand dollars for design, materials, and labor, establishing a model for other growers considering conversion to drip technology. With attention to water quality, routine maintenance, and fertigation scheduling, row crop drip systems can deliver consistent yields using substantially less water than surface irrigation.
nmsu-w-102:
This workbook assists Extension agents, NRCS field offices, and private consultants in helping individuals understand irrigation water analysis reports. The tool evaluates water quality elements and identifies limitations associated with irrigation water sources while calculating leaching fraction based on user inputs. Microsoft Excel 2007 is required, with macro security preferences enabled.
Users input laboratory test results directly into the Report tab. Key analyses include pH, electrical conductivity, calcium, magnesium, sodium, chloride, boron, sulfate, and bicarbonate. Results can be entered in either milliequivalents per liter or milligrams per liter units. Orange-highlighted cells indicate areas requiring user input. Complete and consistent entry is essential for accurate interpretation.
The workbook contains dedicated sections addressing different water quality concerns. For salinity assessment, laboratories may report either total dissolved solids or electrical conductivity. A conversion factor multiplies EC by 640 for water with EC between 0.1 and 5.0 mmhos per centimeter to estimate TDS in milligrams per liter, while EC greater than 5.0 mmhos per centimeter is multiplied by 800. These conversions allow users to translate between reporting conventions and compare against published tolerance tables.
Infiltration concerns are evaluated using the sodium adsorption ratio, calculated from calcium, magnesium, and sodium concentrations. An adjusted SAR incorporates bicarbonate content to account for calcium precipitation under high-bicarbonate conditions. The permeability hazard assessment references a lookup table based on both SAR and electrical conductivity values. Low-salinity water paired with high sodium tends to degrade soil structure, while higher-salinity water with balanced sodium can actually support better infiltration.
For specific ion effects, bicarbonate, boron, chloride, and sodium receive individual consideration due to their potential negative impacts on plant growth. The workbook calculates pounds of nutrient added per acre-inch of water for sulfate, nitrate, and potassium, supporting fertilizer management decisions. This function is particularly useful when irrigation water carries a substantial nutrient load that can be credited toward crop fertility requirements.
The leaching fraction section allows users to select crop and location combinations from a dropdown menu, linking to estimated water consumption and soil salinity thresholds where 10 percent yield reduction occurs. Users must identify the appropriate soil EC value for their selected crop or enter current soil test results. High-frequency systems like drip and sprinkler irrigation require different leaching fraction calculations than low-frequency systems using gated pipe, border dikes, or flooding methods.
Normal irrigation water pH ranges from 6.5 to 8.4. Electrical conductivity values below 0.25 mmhos per centimeter indicate excellent water quality, while values exceeding 3.0 mmhos per centimeter may present problems. Total dissolved solids below 450 milligrams per liter generally pose no restrictions, though values above 2,000 milligrams per liter can create severe limitations on many crops.
For infiltration evaluation, water with sodium adsorption ratio below 3 should have no use restrictions. Problems increase when SAR ranges between 3 and 9, while SAR above 9 may cause severe soil limitations. Trees, vines, and woody ornamentals typically show sodium sensitivity, though annual crops tolerate higher sodium levels. Sodium and chloride can cause leaf burn when evaporation is high, humidity is low, and temperatures are elevated, particularly when applied via overhead sprinklers.
Boron tolerance varies significantly by crop. Blackberries are very sensitive at less than 0.5 milligrams per liter, while cherries, pecans, and grapes tolerate 0.5 to 0.75 milligrams per liter. Sorghum, tomato, and alfalfa demonstrate tolerance up to 6.0 milligrams per liter. Boron is not easily leached from soil, so continued irrigation with boron-rich water accumulates over seasons and can reach toxic levels even when individual applications seem moderate.
The plugging potential section addresses drip irrigation concerns. Water should contain less than 50 milligrams per liter suspended solids, with 100 milligrams per liter or higher creating severe limitations. Bacterial counts greater than 50,000 per milliliter can cause severe plugging if left untreated. Hydrogen sulfide presence above 0.1 milligrams per liter may encourage sulfur bacteria growth that forms slime.
Iron and manganese levels should remain below 0.1 milligrams per liter. These minerals can promote iron bacteria growth, producing brown slime that corrodes plumbing and clogs emitters. Severe limitations develop at levels above 1.5 milligrams per liter. Bicarbonate below 1.5 milliequivalents per liter causes no problems, though severe issues develop above 2.5 milliequivalents per liter due to calcium carbonate scale precipitation at pH above 7.5.
The publication emphasizes that estimating the leaching fraction for a given crop in a given area of New Mexico requires understanding tolerable soil salinity levels. Users benefit from conducting soil tests to establish baseline conditions before implementing management decisions. Consumptive water use estimates combine with calculated leaching fractions to determine total water requirements for maintaining desired salinity conditions under specific irrigation system types. By working through the workbook systematically, growers and advisors can identify water quality constraints early and design irrigation and soil management practices that avoid long-term productivity loss.
nmsu-cr-477:
A growing number of communities are revising zoning regulations to permit residents to maintain small poultry flocks as either hobbies or income sources. Raising chickens, turkeys, geese, or ducks in populated areas requires proper animal care and considerate neighbor relations. Before obtaining any birds, research your local zoning guidelines and obtain necessary permits, as requirements vary significantly by location.
For rural settings, limiting a laying flock to approximately 50 hens is advisable, though there is little advantage in having more than 18 to 20 layers. A flock of this magnitude should provide one egg daily per person for a family of five or six. The general recommendation suggests maintaining 12 baby chicks and keeping three layers for each member of the family. Urban and suburban residents often face stricter limitations, sometimes restricted to fewer than ten hens, which may prove uneconomical due to feed expenses.
Prospective owners can either purchase started pullets, young hens aged six to twenty weeks, or begin with baby chicks. Started pullets commence egg production between twenty and twenty-four weeks of age, offering faster returns than raising chicks from hatching. When selecting chicks, confirm they originate from a pullorum-free flock operating under the National Poultry Improvement Plan and request Marek's disease vaccination before shipment.
Chicken breeds are specifically developed for either egg production, meat production, or dual purposes. For maximum egg productivity with minimal feed waste, leghorn-type birds and their inbred crosses perform optimally, producing more eggs on less feed per dozen than chickens bred for meat production. Broiler chickens suitable for meat derive from crosses of White Cornish males with New Hampshire or White Plymouth Rock females. Dual-purpose breeds such as Rhode Island Reds and Plymouth Rocks produce fewer eggs than leghorns but yield more meat when culled.
Mature birds require 2.5 to 3 square feet per bird, while chicks and pullets need less space initially. Most farms possess suitable locations for housing twenty-five to one hundred chickens, though municipal ordinances typically impose stricter limits. Homemade feeders and waterers can be constructed from common materials like PVC pipes, eliminating expensive equipment purchases.
Before chick arrival, thoroughly clean and disinfect housing using appropriate disinfectants available from hatcheries or feed stores. Ensure facilities are completely dry before adding litter. Good ventilation without drafts is critical to prevent respiratory illness, and secure housing keeps out predators and rodents.
Brooding systems vary from commercial electric, oil, or gas units to homemade alternatives. One effective homemade option utilizes a light bulb for heat within a box measuring twenty-five by twenty inches, with a cloth curtain for temperature adjustment. Hang a 60- to 100-watt light bulb through the top cover, shielded by a tin can hover, maintaining an 18 inches from the litter minimum distance via chain suspension.
Starting brooder temperatures should be 90 to 95 degrees Fahrenheit at floor level near the hover, decreasing approximately five degrees weekly as chicks mature. Materials such as sawdust, wood shavings, straw, and peanut hulls serve as suitable litter, applied four to six inches deep. Maintain dry litter conditions and prevent crowding, as each chick needs 1 square foot of floor space. Watch chick behavior to judge temperature: chicks huddled directly under the heat source are too cold, while chicks pushed to the far edges are too warm.
Feed chicks commercial starter mash or crumbles immediately upon placement under heat. Provide one end slightly under the hover to encourage eating. After approximately eight weeks, transition chicks to growing mash or crumbles. When pullets begin laying around twenty weeks of age, switch to layer ration containing elevated calcium levels necessary for strong eggshell development.
For layers, the simplest way to feed a small flock of chickens is to purchase a complete ration, whether mash, pellets, or crumbles, from a feed store. Maintain continuous access to balanced nutrition, as complete feeds provide nutritionally balanced diets for your birds.
Water quality and availability prove critical. Keep plenty of clean, fresh water available for the layers and adjust waterers to shoulder height to minimize litter moisture around containers. In hot weather, water consumption can double, and shortage quickly reduces egg production.
Vaccinate flocks against Newcastle disease, bronchitis, and fowl pox. Request Marek's disease vaccination when ordering chicks from hatcheries. Beak trimming prevents cannibalism and may be performed at day-old and approximately sixteen weeks of age using sharp equipment or electric trimming machines. Maintain a clean environment, dispose of dead birds promptly, and limit visitors to reduce disease introduction.
Turkeys, geese, and ducks require similar basic facilities but different nutritional provisions. Young turkeys often respond well to having bright-colored marbles in the drinking water to encourage initial eating and drinking. Geese and ducks should receive crumbled or pelleted feed rather than whole grains, which can cause choking. Waterfowl also need access to water deep enough to submerge their heads for proper cleaning of nostrils and eyes.
Regular coop maintenance significantly reduces neighbor complaints. Clean and disinfect housing at least weekly, removing waste and feathers completely. Composting litter and dead birds following proper protocols prevents odor accumulation. Most importantly, limit your flock to female chickens or ducks, as roosters, guinea fowl, and geese generate considerable noise disruption in populated areas.
nmsu-h-149:
Marketing holds significant importance for small-scale agricultural producers. Farmers increasingly want to take control of selling their own products, making it essential to understand the various marketing pathways available. The following represents the primary marketing channels that individual agricultural producers should evaluate.
Commission agents, commonly called brokers or aggregators, negotiate contracts between producers and buyers. These intermediaries earn compensation either through percentage commissions tied to sales prices or weights, or through flat fees per unit sold. They can open access to larger wholesale markets a single farm could not reach alone, but reduce the per-unit return to the grower.
Community-supported agriculture, or CSA, connects producers directly with local consumers. Under this model, consumers subscribe to receive regular harvests from a specific farm throughout the growing season, creating a direct relationship between grower and customer. CSA provides predictable cash flow early in the season through subscription payments and shares the risk of weather or pest problems with the consumer base.
Convenience stores offer another outlet where producers sell products to locally owned establishments that subsequently resell them. These retailers may operate independently or function as components of larger business complexes such as service stations.
Farm and roadside stands allow producers to sell harvested goods directly to consumers at fixed locations, typically on the farm property or along major roads. These operations can range from simple setups using pickup trucks to sophisticated retail facilities. The primary goal involves building a base of repeat customers. Signage, consistent hours, and reliable quality are key to establishing loyalty.
Farmers' markets provide venues where multiple producers sell products simultaneously, usually in populated areas with established regulations. Markets vary in format, including year-round operations, state-sponsored markets, and producer associations. Producers typically must meet the market's rules on what can be sold, such as grown-not-resold requirements, and pay booth or membership fees.
Food processors typically work through forward contracts, purchasing crops that they transform into different product forms before reselling. Some processors also purchase products directly at harvest without prior agreements. Forward contracts lock in a price and outlet but require the grower to meet specified volume and quality targets.
Food service operations include restaurants, charitable organizations, food cooperatives, and institutional cafeterias in schools, universities, and hospitals. Producers sell directly to these end users who incorporate products into menus and meal services. Chefs often value specialty and heirloom items, while institutional buyers emphasize consistent supply and competitive pricing.
Grocery stores purchase from producers through their purchasing agents and distribute products across retail locations using established distribution systems. Some stores accept direct delivery to individual outlets, and successful producers may expand their product lines into related items. Meeting liability insurance, labeling, and food-safety requirements is usually necessary.
Pick-your-own operations, sometimes called U-PIK or PYO, allow customers to harvest products directly from fields. This approach eliminates harvest labor costs and reduces expenses related to sorting, storage, packaging, and shipping. Suitable crops include berries, tree fruits, pumpkins, chile, tomatoes, and Christmas trees. Some growers use this as a secondary outlet after the initial commercial harvest.
Wholesalers purchase products outright and distribute them to various retail outlets. They specialize in categories including specialty crops, organically grown foods, and ornamental plants. Wholesalers take ownership of the risk of resale but generally pay lower prices than direct-to-consumer channels.
Online marketing and mail order utilize digital platforms including social media networks and farm websites. Producers use these tools to interact directly with customers, promoting their businesses and generating sales opportunities. Producers may also employ combinations of these approaches simultaneously to maximize income and market reach.
Successful implementation requires intentional marketing management. Producers must identify consumer needs, develop products meeting those needs profitably, create marketing plans, execute sales, and provide customer service. Effective marketing plans contain four essential components: analyzing market opportunities, selecting appropriate markets, developing suitable product mixes, and controlling marketing efforts.
When analyzing market opportunities, producers should consider whether the marketing plan fits their existing operation and whether it will increase income or provide primary support. Financial capacity matters significantly, requiring assessment of investment capabilities for facilities and equipment, including borrowed funds if necessary. Location quality affects success, as does the ability to produce sufficient quantities meeting consumer demands.
Labor availability requires evaluation. Producers should identify whether existing labor suffices or whether hiring becomes necessary. Identifying individual competitive advantages proves important, whether through location, available labor pools, operational quality, or specialized growing expertise. Most critically, producers must genuinely possess the ability and willingness to serve public needs.
Market selection depends substantially on personal preferences, comfort levels, and capacity to meet demand. Transportation, facilities, location, and distance to markets all influence decisions.
Developing appropriate product mixes involves ensuring the right products appear at correct prices in suitable locations at optimal times in usable forms. When executed properly, customers purchase at agreed prices and both parties achieve satisfaction. Promotional strategies include local media advertising, online promotion, and word-of-mouth referrals. Distribution channel selection depends on customer identity and location, choosing methods that deliver the freshest products most effectively.
Controlling marketing efforts requires regular observation and reflection on system performance. Producers should assess whether improvements are possible, evaluate attention to details, monitor customer reactions, research competitor pricing, and inspect product condition before sale.
Food safety compliance with the Food Safety Modernization Act has become a critical marketing consideration. Producers can access information through the Produce Safety Alliance website and NMSU Extension resources, particularly Guide E-510 addressing food business startup requirements in New Mexico. The New Mexico Department of Agriculture serves as the authoritative regulatory agency for food safety information.
nmsu-cr-701:
Late frosts represent the primary challenge to fruit production in central and northern New Mexico. Fruit crops bloom at different times depending on the species: apricots in March, peaches in late March through April, and apples in early to mid-April. Bloom dates fluctuate slightly from year to year. Growers and home gardeners in northern New Mexico typically must wait until mid-May to determine whether they will have a viable crop.
Three general categories of freezing conditions exist based on atmospheric circumstances. An advection freeze occurs when a large cold, dry air mass moves into an area with strong winds, typically arctic weather originating from Canada or the West Coast. This type affects entire regions or states, and specific site locations or management approaches offer minimal benefit. A radiation freeze happens when a local cold air mass settles in an area with little wind or cloud cover. These freezes are common in New Mexico, particularly in northern valleys where cold air drains downward into adjacent valleys on clear nights. Site selection and management practices can minimize damage from radiation freezes. An advection-radiation freeze combines characteristics of both types and is the most damaging of the three.
Flower development charts for apricot, peach, nectarine, and apple indicate frost management practices based on forecasted temperatures. If temperatures will reach the ten percent damage threshold, action may not be necessary, as some bud and flower damage aids natural thinning and potentially improves the fruit crop. However, when temperatures will drop into the fifty to ninety percent damage threshold range, protective measures are essential.
Flower buds demonstrate less hardiness than vegetative buds, while flowers in bloom and fruitlets are more vulnerable than buds in tight or balloon stages. As flower buds develop, their hardiness decreases. Within a flower, the pistil represents the most sensitive component, followed by stamens and then petals.
A razor blade and magnifying glass can examine buds and flowers for frost damage. Pistil length serves as a rough indicator of freeze timing. Pistils measuring two millimeters or less typically experienced winter freeze damage; elongated but relatively short pistils indicate earlier damage; pistils nearly matching healthy flower length suggest recent damage. When small fruits measuring one to one and one-quarter centimeters in diameter sustain frost damage, they display shrunken surfaces with brown seeds and typically fall off following the freeze event.
Frosts can be managed through active or passive strategies. Growers can actively select favorable orchard sites to avoid late frost damage, positioning orchards at least fifty feet above valley bottoms on slopes of six to eight degrees for proper air drainage, or adjacent to large bodies of water that moderate temperature changes. Budget constraints and irrigation water availability often limit site selection options.
Several late frost management techniques exist with variable effectiveness across different locations. Wind machines function appropriately for radiation frosts in orchard settings, potentially increasing temperatures by two to four degrees Fahrenheit. Their effectiveness diminishes when temperatures drop to twenty-two degrees or below. Wind machines work only when strong inversion layers exist with significantly warmer air twenty to thirty feet above the orchard compared to ground-level temperatures.
Heaters and smudge pots offer portability suitable for backyard trees, orchards, or high tunnels. A minimum quantity is required for effectiveness depending on temperature, orchard setting, and tree density, though fuel costs warrant consideration.
Overhead sprinklers provide effective frost protection but require continuous water supply and correct nozzle sizing and pressure. The latent heat released as water freezes on plant tissue holds the coating near thirty-two degrees Fahrenheit. High frost frequency situations necessitate consideration of soil nutrient leaching.
Under-canopy sprinklers provide limited protection and work best combined with wind machines or regular irrigation systems. High tunnels offer limited protection. When late frosts frequently occur with temperatures below twenty-five degrees, additional heating inside the high tunnel becomes necessary for flower and fruitlet protection.
Row covers and light bulbs provide limited protection, suitable primarily for home gardeners. Christmas lights with seven-watt incandescent bulbs, not LEDs, can be used because they emit meaningful radiant heat. Homeowners with few fruit trees might try them with tarp or plastic covers over trees.
Barbecue grills or stoves assist when the late frost season approaches its end and homeowners wish to preserve one or two backyard fruit trees. Placing grills on low settings when temperatures become critically low and running them overnight with plastic or tarp coverings over trees may save fruit. Sprayable compounds under development aim to delay bloom timing. These remain in trial stages, with commercial product registration anticipated.
Weather forecast information proves critical for frost management. Growers should regularly compare temperatures at their own sites with local forecasts and use predictions most similar to their location. GPS-based weather channels and smartphone applications provide accurate, precise temperature forecasts. Commercial growers benefit greatly from on-site low temperature alarm systems that indicate when frost protection actions must commence.
